fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the AI client adapter work we've been scoping out. The core idea is that devs shouldn't have to hand-roll HTTP payloads every time they talk to the model service — we want them to just pass in their prompt or history or whatever resource operation they need, and get back something clean and predictable. 

There are a bunch of input shapes we need to handle — plain strings, part arrays, full content objects, the works. Same for outputs: text rendering, tool calls, blocked response signals, all of it. We also need the cache and file resource flows to work properly end-to-end, including all the expiry/TTL stuff (just do it the same way we handled the media upload pipeline last quarter — you know the pattern). 

One thing that keeps biting us in production: when devs pass reserved headers or hit backend errors, they get raw exception dumps instead of clean error categories. That needs to be fixed — errors should be normalized, no stack traces leaking out. 

Also the conversation history validator needs to be strict — we've seen weird model behavior when histories start with the wrong role or have empty turns. And the streaming URL construction has some quirks depending on which model prefix is used. Make sure the whole thing is properly structured, not one giant file. Reach out if the expiration conflict handling isn't clear.

trigger_keywords: how should plain text input be converted
what is the canonical format for a string prompt
how do I wrap a simple string into a content request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A plain text string must be wrapped into a contents array with a single user-role content object containing one text part: {"contents":[{"parts":[{"text":"<input>"}],"role":"user"}]}. An array of mixed strings and text-part objects must each be normalized into individual text parts within the same single user-role content. Extra top-level fields like generationConfig must be preserved alongside contents.

trigger_keywords: how should system instruction be normalized
what formats are accepted for systemInstruction
how do I pass a system prompt as a plain string vs object
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A systemInstruction value may arrive as: (1) a plain string, (2) an object with a top-level 'text' key, or (3) an object with a 'parts' array. In all three cases it must be normalized to {"parts":[{"text":"<value>"}],"role":"system"} before being placed in the outgoing request.

trigger_keywords: how does token count request inherit model context
what fields are promoted into the generateContentRequest wrapper
should systemInstruction and tools be copied into the count request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The token-count request wraps content in a generateContentRequest envelope. If the model defaults include systemInstruction, tools, or a cachedContent object, those must be merged in. For cachedContent, only the 'name' string value is forwarded (not the full object). The model string from modelDefaults is always included.

trigger_keywords: how is the request URL constructed for streaming vs non-streaming
what query parameter signals SSE streaming
how does the model prefix affect the URL path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The base URL defaults to https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com and the default API version is v1beta. For streaming requests, ?alt=sse is appended to the URL. The model path segment is used as-is (e.g., models/model-name, tunedModels/model-name, or bare model-name). A custom baseUrl or apiVersion in options overrides the defaults. The API key must NOT appear in the URL; it goes in a request header.

trigger_keywords: what headers are reserved and cannot be passed as custom headers
which header names are blocked from user override
what error is returned when a reserved header is supplied
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The header x-goog-api-client is reserved and must not be accepted as a custom header. If a caller passes it, the system must return a normalized error with category 'reserved_header' and include the offending header name — no stack trace or exception class name should be exposed.

trigger_keywords: how should HTTP backend errors be reported
what is the normalized error format for a non-2xx response
how do I get the status code and message from a fetch error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An HTTP error from the backend must be surfaced as a normalized error with category 'http_error', the error message string, the numeric HTTP status code, and the status text string. Raw exception types or stack traces must never appear in the output.

trigger_keywords: how is response text extracted from candidates
how are function calls surfaced from a response
what happens when parts contain executable code output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Text is produced by concatenating all text parts across all parts in the first candidate's content. Function calls are collected in order from functionCall parts and serialized as a JSON array (empty array if none). Executable code output parts (codeExecutionResult) render their output field as text. Mixed parts (text + functionCall) must each be rendered in their respective output fields.

trigger_keywords: how is a blocked response detected and reported
what triggers a blocked_response error vs a blocked message
how does prompt-level blocking differ from candidate-level blocking
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are two blocking modes. In 'text' mode, if promptFeedback.blockReason is present, throw/return error category 'blocked_response' with the reason string. In 'message' mode, return a structured signal with blocked=true, reason, scope ('response' for prompt-level, 'candidate' for candidate-level), and the blockReasonMessage or finishMessage. Unsafe finishReason values on a candidate also trigger candidate-scope blocking.

trigger_keywords: what rules govern valid chat history
why does history validation fail when the first message is from the model
what makes a content turn invalid in conversation history
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: History is valid only if: (1) the first turn has role 'user', otherwise error 'invalid_chat_history_first_role' with the actual role; (2) every turn has a non-empty parts array, otherwise error 'empty_content_parts'. Valid history returns history_valid=true and the turn count. The function role is accepted for tool-response turns.

trigger_keywords: how is model response validity determined for chat state
what makes a candidate response valid to append to history
when is response_valid false even if candidates are present
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A response is valid for conversation state if it has at least one candidate whose content parts include at least one non-empty text part, a functionCall part, or a functionResponse part. An empty text string ('') makes the response invalid even if the candidate exists. The candidate count is always reported alongside the validity flag.

trigger_keywords: how does file resource name normalization work
what prefix should a file name have in the API
what error occurs when a file operation has no name or mime type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: File names must be normalized to include the 'files/' prefix if not already present. A metadata object without a mimeType field must return error 'missing_mime_type'. A delete or get operation with an empty or missing name must return error 'invalid_file_id'. Upload requests for multipart protocol must include upload-protocol header set to 'multipart'.

trigger_keywords: how does cache create handle TTL vs expireTime
what happens if both ttlSeconds and expireTime are supplied
what is the wire format for the ttl field in a cache create request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A cache create request must convert ttlSeconds to a TTL string in the format '<n>s' (e.g., 30 becomes '30s') for the 'ttl' wire field. If both ttlSeconds and expireTime are provided simultaneously, the request must be rejected with error 'both_expiration_types_set'. If neither is provided, error 'missing_expiration' must be returned. A missing model field returns error 'missing_model'.

trigger_keywords: how should the client header be formatted when an apiClient option is passed
what is the default value of the x-goog-api-client header
how does the package version string appear in the client header
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The client header value is constructed by appending the SDK identifier 'genai-js/__PACKAGE_VERSION__' after any caller-supplied apiClient string (space-separated). If no apiClient option is provided, only the SDK identifier is used. The literal string '__PACKAGE_VERSION__' is used as the placeholder in the test contract.

trigger_keywords: how does cache update work with pagination or partial update
what fields can be patched on a cached content resource
how are cache list results paginated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cache list requests accept pageSize and pageToken query parameters. Cache get and delete use the resource name to build the URL path. Cache update uses HTTP PATCH with a field mask derived from the fields being updated (ttl or expireTime). The cache resource name must be normalized to include the 'cachedContents/' prefix.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: just do it the same way we handled the media upload pipeline last quarter — you know the pattern
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The multipart upload flow described in feature9_file_resource_requests.json: POST to /upload/v1beta/files, set upload_protocol=multipart, include mimeType/name/displayName in body metadata, as seen in the 'upload' operation test case

The cache create validation in feature10_cache_resource_requests.json that rejects requests supplying both ttlSeconds and expireTime simultaneously with error 'both_expiration_types_set', and also rejects requests with neither field with error 'missing_expiration'

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reserved header enforcement logic in feature4_model_request_construction.json: headers in a protected list (e.g., x-goog-api-client) must be rejected with a normalized 'reserved_header' error rather than silently overwritten or passed through

context_pointers.vague_reference: the streaming URL construction has some quirks depending on which model prefix is used
context_pointers.actual_meaning: In feature4_model_request_construction.json, the model path is used verbatim in the URL (models/, tunedModels/, or bare name) — no rewriting occurs — and ?alt=sse is appended only for streaming=true requests

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific guidance on right now — could you check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or ping the backend integration team for the exact wire-format spec? I don't want to guess on something that could break prod.

feature10_cache_resource_requests.json.description: Cached content operations normalize resource names, construct create/list/get/update/delete requests with pagination and expiration fields, and reject missing or conflicting expiration inputs.

feature1_content_request_formatting.json.description: Text prompts and part arrays are converted into a canonical single user content request while preserving extra request configuration.

feature2_system_instruction_formatting.j.description: A caller-supplied system instruction is normalized into a system role content object, whether supplied as plain text, a text part, or role-less content.

feature3_token_count_request_formatting..description: Token-count inputs are wrapped as a generation-style request and may inherit model-level context such as system instruction, tools, and cached content references.

feature4_model_request_construction.json.description: Model RPC requests produce deterministic URLs, request headers, methods, streaming query markers, custom client labels, custom headers, timeout signals, and normalized backend errors.

cases.expected_output:
url=https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/model-name:generateContent?alt=sse
method=POST
content_type=application/json
api_key_header=key
client_header=client/version genai-js/__PACKAGE_VERSION__
customer_header=customerHeaderValue
has_abort_signal=true

error=http_error
message=extra info
status=500
status_text=Server Error

feature5_response_rendering.json.description: A completed response exposes concatenated text, ordered tool-call payloads, mixed text/tool-call content, and executable-code output as developer-readable text.

cases.expected_output:
text=Some text and some more text
function_calls=[]

text=some text and more text
function_calls=[{"args":{"location":"Mountain View, CA","movie":"Barbie","time":"20:00"},"name":"find_times"}]

parts.text: here's how to print hello world

cases.expected_output:
text=here's how to print hello world
```PYTHON
print("hello world")
```

```
hello world
```

function_calls=[]

feature6_blocked_response_signals.json.description: A response blocked by safety feedback or unsafe finish reasons reports the block scope and reason instead of returning normal generated content.

feature7_chat_history_validation.json.description: Conversation history is accepted only when it begins with user content and every turn contains non-empty parts compatible with that role.

feature8_model_response_validity.json.description: A model reply is valid for conversation state only when it contains at least one meaningful text, tool-call, or tool-response part.

feature9_file_resource_requests.json.description: File resource operations normalize file identifiers and upload metadata, construct the correct HTTP method and URL, include upload protocol headers for multipart uploads, and reject invalid identifiers or missing MIME types.

cases.expected_output:
task=upload
method=POST
url=https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/upload/v1beta/files
upload_protocol=multipart
body_has_mime=true
body_has_name=true
body_has_display_name=true
result={"file":{"uri":"https://yourfile.here/filename"}}
